did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses the Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) algorithm, a computational intelligence/optimization technique, for the automatic detection, segmentation, and classification of skin tumour lesions (melanoma vs. benign) from dermoscopy images. It extracts features using multiresolution analysis and applies the ABC technique to enhance performance in categorizing skin cancer lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the application of the Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) algorithm for the automatic detection, segmentation, and classification of skin tumour lesions. It includes a Method section describing the proposed technique, a Results and Discussion section with experimental outcomes (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) on specific datasets (PH2, ISBI), and a Conclusion. Although published in the Journal of Physics: Conference Series, it reports new technical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Analysis of Automatic Detection of Tumour Lesions Images using Bee Colony Technique
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated technique for the detection and classification of skin tumour lesions, specifically distinguishing between melanoma and benign nevi, using the Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) algorithm. The method employs multiresolution analysis for feature extraction and utilizes the ABC optimization technique to enhance segmentation and classification performance. Experimental validation was conducted on images from the PH2 database and the ISBI challenge datasets. The system's efficacy was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics. The results indicate that the proposed bee colony-based approach achieves superior performance compared to existing methods, demonstrating high accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity in skin cancer categorization.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection and segmentation. The study explicitly states it selected 'fifteen melanoma skin lesion images from the PH2 database' and categorizes skin tumours as 'benign or malignant'. Figure 2 and Figure 3 show 'malignant melanoma skin lesion images'.

The comparator class to melanoma. The abstract and introduction mention categorizing images as 'melanoma or benign' or 'healthy and unhealthy nevi'. Figure 1 block diagram includes labels 'not mel' and 'B\P' (Benign) alongside 'MM' (Melanoma).

Mentioned in the Introduction and Conclusion as a potential category ('categorize the image as melanoma or benign or suspension'), but the experimental results and dataset description focus on the binary distinction between melanoma and benign/healthy nevi.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin tumour lesions into 'benign' (healthy nevi) or 'malignant' (melanoma) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is 'cataloguing of images' and 'categorization of skin tumour's as benign or malignant'. The method involves feature extraction followed by classification algorithms. Table 1 reports classification metrics including Accuracy, Sensitivity, and Specificity. The system aims to classify images as 'melanoma or benign or suspension', though experimental validation focuses on the binary distinction between melanoma and benign/healthy nevi.

all_task_types.target: Segmentation masks for skin tumour lesions.
all_task_types.details: The paper mentions 'automatic detection, segmentation, and classification' and states that 'segmentation step can be utilized to enhance the handling of the information'. It discusses using ABC for segmentation and shows 'before and after segmentation' images. However, the primary reported performance metrics (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity) are for the classification task, making classification the primary task.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Database
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy image database used for experimental validation. The study specifically selected images from this database for testing the proposed bee colony technique.

use.testing: Used for experimental validation of the automatic detection and segmentation of malignant melanoma skin lesions.

datasets_used.name: ISBI Challenge Datasets
datasets_used.description: Datasets from the ISBI Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection challenge (years 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019). Mentioned as a source from which images of different skin lesions can be taken for classification.

use.other: Mentioned as a potential source for images of different skin lesions to be classified, but specific experimental counts or usage details (training/testing splits) for these datasets are not reported in the text.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics without statistical significance testing.

PH2 Database.notes: The study randomly selected fifteen melanoma skin lesion images from the PH2 database for experimentation. The results (Accuracy: 0.98, Sensitivity: 0.93, Specificity: 0.91) are attributed to the proposed Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) technique. The paper states these outcomes are from the authors' previous work [11] or current implementation on this dataset, noting 'utmost progressive performance' compared to ground truth.

ISBI Challenge Datasets (2016-2019).notes: The paper mentions that images for classification can be taken from ISBI challenge datasets (2016, 2017, 2018, 2019). However, it does not report specific quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) obtained from these datasets in this study. It only lists them as a source for potential images.

Comparison with Previous Works (Table 1).notes: Table 1 presents a comparison of previous works. The proposed method (Mohanad Aljanabi et.al) shows higher accuracy (0.98) compared to other listed methods. Note: The results for 'Mohanad Aljanabi et.al (2019)' are likely the same as the proposed method in this paper, as the authors cite their own prior work [11] which matches the current paper's claimed performance.

architecture_used.main_model: Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) algorithm used for segmentation and feature selection, combined with multiresolution analysis for feature extraction and standard classification algorithms (e.g., SVM, as referenced in comparison works, though specific classifier for the proposed method is not explicitly named beyond 'several well-known algorithms').

architecture_details.segmentation_method: ABC technique with median filter for boundary detection

training_details.training_method: Empirical parameter selection for ABC optimization
training_details.dataset_split: 15 melanoma images from PH2 database used for experimentation; specific training/testing split not reported for the proposed method in this text

classification_or_output_head.classifier_type: Not explicitly specified (described as 'several well-known algorithms')

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Feed-forward back propagation artificial neural network
R-Surface and LBP features with SVM classifier
Fusion of soft max and SVM classifier
preprocessing_techniques: Median filter applied for boundary detection during segmentation
Multiresolution analysis used for feature extraction
Random selection of images from the PH2 database (15 melanoma images)
Segmentation using ABC technique to isolate lesion boundaries
conclusions: The proposed Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) technique for automatic detection, segmentation, and classification of skin tumour lesions achieves high performance, with reported accuracy of 0.98, sensitivity of 0.93, and specificity of 0.91 on the PH2 database.
The bee colony-based approach demonstrates superior performance compared to several existing methods listed in the study, including those using sparse coding, DCNNs, and other SVM-based classifiers.
The ABC algorithm effectively handles challenging melanoma images characterized by fuzzy borders, low contrast, hairs, and inhomogeneous borders.
The study acknowledges that dermatological injuries often depend on minor databases, which hinders the overview of ABC models.

The authors note that while pre- and post-processing stages can improve accuracy, these additional approaches must be thoroughly analyzed to ensure they do not compromise results when applied to datasets other than those initially tested.
important_remarks: The experimental validation is extremely limited, relying on only 15 randomly selected melanoma images from the PH2 database, which is insufficient for robust performance estimation.
The study acknowledges that dermatological research often depends on minor databases, which hinders the general overview and validation of ABC models.
The paper does not report specific quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) for the ISBI Challenge datasets (2016-2019), despite mentioning them as a potential source for images.
The classifier type used in conjunction with the ABC algorithm is not explicitly specified, described only as 'several well-known algorithms'.
The ABC algorithm parameters (e.g., number of scout bees, cycles) were determined empirically rather than through a systematic optimization or sensitivity analysis.

The authors warn that adding pre- and post-processing stages may compromise results when applied to datasets other than those initially tested, indicating potential data leakage or overfitting risks if not carefully managed.

The comparison with previous works in Table 1 includes the authors' own prior work (Aljanabi et al., 2019) with a much larger dataset (2100 images) compared to the current study's 15 images, making direct performance comparison misleading.
The paper lacks statistical significance testing (p-values, confidence intervals) to support claims of superior performance over existing methods.
